actions.thought: 72 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Playing it from under-the-gun (UTG), even in a 4-handed game, is a significantly negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a more profitable opportunity.

6-4 offsuit is a marginal hand at best - it ranks among the weakest holdings in poker. Despite being on the button with positional advantage, I'm facing two opponents (the blinds) and the hand lacks both high-card value and decent connectivity. At the beginning of a 150-hand session where I need to maximize value over time, folding weak hands early is essential for bankroll health. There will be plenty of better opportunities to accumulate chips without playing hands this weak. This disciplined approach sets the tone for solid play throughout the session.

Preflop in SB with A♠3♠ vs BB only after folds from UTG and Button. A3s is a strong suited ace hand with flush potential, wheel straight potential, and ace blocker. Deep stacks (~100BB). Standard play is to raise for initiative, isolation, and fold equity against BB's wide defending range. Limping OOP is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots or strong BB raises. Folding wastes position and hand equity. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a balanced size: builds pot when ahead, applies pressure, and maintains fold equity. OOP postflop playability is good with suited ace.

Facing a raise to 300 from the SB I need to call 200 into a 400 pot (~33% equity required). My cards (5h3d) are unsuited, unconnected and will be dominated by much of the SB’s opening range; they have poor playability and few realizable equity or blocker benefits. A 3-bet here is unjustified. Even with postflop position, this hand isn’t worth defending at these prices — fold and preserve the stack.

8-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. From UTG (early position), I have no business playing this hand. I would be first to act post-flop with a hand that is dominated by most reasonable holdings. The cost-to-benefit ratio is terrible - I'm risking 100 chips from the worst position at the table with minimal equity potential. This is a clear fold regardless of stack size or game conditions.

73o is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity realization, and disconnected ranks offsuit. Even on the button facing only the BB after folds and dead SB money, this spot doesn't warrant entering the pot. Pot odds are decent (~2.5:1 for a call), but the hand plays poorly postflop in position. Raising would be a bluff with bad blockers and postflop potential. Folding preserves stack for superior spots in a long 150-hand session to maximize EV.

I’m in the small blind with 6d8d — a playable suited connector heads-up but out of position postflop. The pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to complete to 100, giving me ~4:1 immediate pot odds. With deep stacks (~98 BB) and reasonable implied odds for straights/flushes, I’d prefer to see a flop cheaply rather than inflating the pot by min-raising out of position. Calling preserves flexibility and keeps the pot controllable; I’ll fold to heavy preflop 3-bets but otherwise play the flop for value or as a draw.

I have Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand. Minni completed from the small blind, which is often a weak range or an attempt to see a cheap flop. I should raise here to build the pot for value and take control of the hand. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard iso-raise in this position.

Call to 400. I have 6♦8♦ — a suited connector with good multi-street play and implied odds heads-up. The pot is 500 and I only need to add 300 (calling to 400 total), so I’m getting ~2.7:1 immediate pot odds (need ~27% equity). With ~98BB effective stacks and a wide raising range from the big blind, this hand has enough equity and playability to see a flop. I’m out of position, so I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize my equity rather than inflate the pot with a marginal 3‑bet; I’ll call and evaluate the flop (and fold to a large 3‑bet).

Board is A♠ T♥ 3♠ and I hold 6♦8♦ — no pair, no spade, only backdoor diamond/runner-runner straight possibilities. The flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (Ax, Tx, sets, spade draws). I’m effectively out of position here (button folded so SB acts first postflop) and stacks are deep (~95BB), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a weak hand and minimal equity. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my equity, and keeps options open to fold to aggression or to bluff/semi-bluff only if a favorable turn comes (a diamond or a card that misses the raiser’s range). A c-bet here would have poor fold equity and risks getting called or raised by better hands, so I check.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on an Ace-high board with some draws present. My pre-flop raise narrowed Minni's range, but she could easily have a weaker Ace or a flush draw. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of 400 (half the pot) is a standard continuation bet size that gets value from weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable.

I’m out of position with 6♦8♦ on A♠ T♥ 3♠. I have no pair, no spade draw and only remote backdoor outs. The pot is 1,200 and Flash’s bet puts me in to call 400 — I need roughly 25% equity to make a call break-even. Against a reasonable betting range here (Ax, Tx, sets, some KQ/KJ spade draws and other value hands), my equity is well under that (single-digit to low-teens at best). Raising would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity OOP and would commit me to tough decisions on later streets with deep stacks. Sunk preflop chips don’t change the math, so the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

4-handed game, UTG position with pocket 3s, a standard opening hand in short-handed play (22+ pairs are strong). Preflop first to act, blinds 50/100, pot 150. Raising is optimal over limping or folding to take initiative, build the pot, and narrow the field. Standard raise size around 3x BB (300 total), consistent with my previous action in Hand #1. Deep stacks support this play; opponents have shown aggression but no reason to deviate from GTO-ish strategy here.

Facing an UTG open to 300 (3x BB) from a typically strong early position range, Q♦7♠ is a marginal, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability. Calling 300 to see a flop is a small % of my stack but invites multiway action (SB/BB still to act) which further reduces my equity and plan. A light reraise with Q7o is too risky — Elon will call or 4-bet enough of the time, and Flash (SB) has shown aggression previously, so I won't get folds often. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position with a stronger hand or clearer fold/raise dynamics.

I am in the Small Blind with Seven-Nine offsuit. Elon raised to 300 from the UTG position. 79o is too weak to call a 3x raise out of position against a tight UTG range, and it is not a strong candidate for a 3-bet bluff. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Early in the session (hand 3 of 150), I'm in the Big Blind with T8o against Elon's UTG raise to 300. After Minni and Flash fold, it's heads-up. I need to call 200 more chips into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 pot odds. T8o has reasonable equity (~35%) against a wide UTG raising range and can make straights or top pair. The pot odds are decent, it's heads-up play (better for marginal hands), and my stack is large enough to take a flop. I'll call and evaluate the flop action, though I'll need to exercise discipline if Elon continues betting aggressively.

Betting 400 chips on the flop with T♦8♠ on a T♥4♠2♥ board. I have top pair with an 8 kicker plus a gutshot straight draw, which is strong on this dry board. The flop texture (low, uncoordinated) limits Elon's likely holdings. A bet builds the pot when I'm ahead, gets value from weaker pairs and draws in his range, and applies pressure. The 400 bet is roughly 2/3 of the current pot and represents a standard value bet sizing. This also sets up good river/turn decisions with a deeper stack."

Facing a flop donk-bet of 400 into a 1050 pot from Claude in the BB after calling my UTG raise preflop. My pocket 3s gives me a weak middle pair on a T-4-2 board with two hearts, vulnerable to any overpair (55+), top pair (Tx), sets (22/44/TT), straights (53+), or flush draws. Claude's donk-bet is polarized towards value (sets, two-pair, trips) or strong draws/semis, with few pure bluffs after calling pre. Pot odds require ~28% equity to call, but my equity is likely <20% against his range due to reverse implied odds and board texture favoring his continuing range. Folding preserves stack; continuation unlikely profitable in position but too weak to float or raise-bluff given history and deep stacks.

As UTG in a 4-handed table with ~95bb, A6s is a reasonable open — it has suitedness and blocker value to Ax hands and plays well postflop. I want initiative and to thin the field, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. I size to ~3x the BB (300 total) to discourage marginal callers and small blind/BTN stealing and to reduce profitable cold 3-bets. Plan: c-bet favorable A/spade/texture flops, pot-control with weak showings, and fold to large, polarized 3-bets from tight players (call smaller 3-bets to play postflop depending on reads).

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). Minni has opened with a raise to 300 from under-the-gun in this 4-handed game. K2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly against an opening range, as it is frequently dominated and lacks post-flop playability. Despite having positional advantage, the hand is not strong enough to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

With 2-8o (deuce-eight offsuit), I hold one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni opened with a raise to 300 from UTG, suggesting a reasonably strong hand. While I'm in the small blind and only need to call 250 more, this hand has virtually no equity and poor playability post-flop. Even with the favorable pot odds, calling with such a weak hand is -EV long-term. I should fold here and preserve my stack for better opportunities. Position and pot odds don't override the fundamental weakness of 2-8o against an aggressor.

27o is one of the worst hands in poker, completely uncoordinated, offsuit, low cards with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown some aggression but this is a standard strong range spot. In BB, getting 2.25:1 odds on the call (200 into 650), but equity against a UTG opening range is around 15-20%, far below break-even, and I'll be OOP postflop with a hand that rarely improves and plays terribly. No fold equity if I raise. Deep stacks mean no need to gamble. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I have 72o, which is arguably the worst starting hand in poker. I am in the UTG position, acting first. There is no reason to play this hand. Folding is the only viable strategic move here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with King-Nine suited (Ks9s), a playable hand in this position. The action folds to me preflop with only 100 to call to see the flop against the two blinds. Position is crucial - being on the button gives me significant postflop advantage. King-Nine suited has good playability with potential to make strong hands (straights, flushes, top pair). Getting 2.5:1 odds and having the best position to act last postflop makes calling the correct play here. This allows me to maximize positional advantage and control the hand postflop with flexibility based on the board texture and opponent actions.

In the small blind with J5 suited facing a button limp (Claude called 100) and big blind behind (Minni), the pot is 250 and I need to call 50 more to complete (total 100 invested). Pot odds are excellent at over 5:1 direct, plus strong implied odds with deep stacks (mine 9650, others ~10k). J5s is playable speculative suited hand that can flop strong draws (flush, gutshots, pairs), and multiway pots favor this type of hand. Raising is unnecessary risk with a marginal hand; folding is too tight given the price. SB OOP is a slight negative but equity realization is feasible postflop if we improve. Standard play: complete and see flop.

I'm in the big blind with A♥J♠ and two limpers (SB and Button) into a 300-chip pot. AJo is strong enough to take the initiative and isolate the limpers or win the pot preflop. I don't want to give a free multiway flop with a hand that plays better heads-up and wants to build value when ahead, so I raise rather than check. I size to 500 total (5xBB) — large enough to apply real fold equity and discourage loose callers, but small relative to our stacks so we keep maneuverability postflop. If I get 3-bet, I'll re-evaluate (often folding AJo to a large, non-isolation 3-bet); if called, I'll plan to c-bet favorable flops and proceed carefully out of position versus two players.

After my initial call of 100 on the button with Ks9s, Minni has 3-bet to 500 from the big blind. I'm now facing 400 more to call.

Current analysis:
- Pot odds: 700:400 = 1.75:1 (getting ~2.75:1 with potential future action)
- Ks9s is a reasonable hand with good equity against Minni's 3-bet range, which likely includes many non-premium hands
- Position advantage: I'm on the button and will act last postflop, giving me significant control
- Opponent analysis: Elon (small blind) is weak; Minni's raise is the main concern
- Hand strength: While not premium, Ks9s plays well postflop with draw potential (flush, straight possibilities)

4-betting with this marginal hand risks unnecessarily bloating the pot out of position for Minni. Folding would be overly conservative given decent odds and my position advantage. Calling exploits my button position, allows me to play profitably postflop based on board texture, and gives me the flexibility to fold easily against further aggression if appropriate. This is the mathematically sound play.

Facing a BB 3-bet squeeze after button limp and SB complete, J5h is too weak to continue OOP multiway. Minni's range is strong for value or semi-bluff squeeze (pairs, broadway, Ax), Claude's flat call indicates decent strength. Pot odds 1100:400 require ~27% equity, but J5s realizes <25% effectively OOP with gappy suited connectors poor postflop playability vs 2 opponents (low pair weak kicker, weak draws). SPR ~6 post-call doesn't help speculative hand. Fold preserves stack for better spots; 146 hands left, deep stacks.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (A♥J♠ on J♣9♣2♠). Button called my sizable preflop raise, so his range is wide (broadways, medium pairs, club draws, suited connectors). The board has two clubs, so there are backdoor and some direct flush draws that I want to charge and deny equity to. Standard play is a continuation bet for value and protection out of position. I size to ~60% of the pot (700 into 1100) to get value from worse jacks, nines, and A-highs while making club draws pay to see a cheap turn. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises large I’ll be cautious (he likely has a set/two pair/strong draw) and will fold to heavy pressure unless the raise is small enough to justify a call for pot odds.

I'm holding Ks9s on a flop of Jc 9c 2s. I have:

Current hand strength:
- Middle pair (9s) with King kicker
- 4-flush (9 remaining spade outs for the flush draw)
- 3 additional overcard outs (Kings), plus 2 outs for another 9 to make trips
- Approximately 14 total outs if behind

Situation analysis:
- Minni's preflop 3-bet range likely includes premium pairs, AK, AQ, AJ, but also weaker holdings
- Minni's flop bet of 700 into 1800 is a modest bet (about 39% of pot), suggesting either a continuation bet or careful value betting rather than a massive overbet
- I'm on the button with excellent position for the turn and river
- To call ratio: 700 to win 2500 (roughly 3.5:1 odds)

This hand has reasonable equity against Minni's range. Against premium pairs I'm behind, but against AK, AQ without the J, or lighter hands, I have equity or are ahead. The flush draw with 9 outs plus overcard outs and positional advantage make this a profitable call. Raising risks bloating the pot while I'm potentially behind; folding is too conservative. Calling allows me to see the turn, control pot growth, and make better decisions based on the next card while maintaining positional advantage.

This is a clear call.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥J♠ on J♣9♣2♠2♦) and we’re heads-up versus Claude who called preflop and my sizable flop c‑bet. His calling range is wide: Jx, 9x, pocket pairs, and club draws — the main hands that now beat me are sets (22, possibly JJ) which are unlikely but must be respected. Checking hands the turn hands the initiative to club draws and lets him realize equity for free; betting charges club draws and extracts value from worse jacks, nines and medium pairs. Pot is 2,500 — a bet of ~1,500 (~60% pot) is large enough to deny equity and get value while keeping the pot manageable given stacks. Plan: bet 1,500 to charge draws and get value. If he check‑raises big (strong set signal) I will fold to heavy pressure; I’ll call smaller raises to evaluate further on the river.

Analyzing the turn situation with Ks9s on a board of Jc9c2s2d:

Current hand strength: I have middle pair (pair of nines) with a King kicker. The turn brought a blank (2d) that doesn't directly complete obvious hands but does pair the board.

Minni's line analysis: His aggressive 3-bet preflop followed by flop bet and now turn bet of 1500 suggests a strong but not necessarily premium range. Given the pot size progression, he's likely betting value with hands like AJ, AK, AQ, strong pairs (JJ+), or continuation betting with draws.

My equity assessment: Against his likely range:
- Against AK/AQ without the J: I'm likely ahead or close
- Against AJ: I'm behind but have significant outs
- Against premium pairs: I'm behind but have 9 spade outs for the flush, 3 Kings, and 2 additional nines
- My total outs are approximately 14 (with 9 clean spade outs)
- Estimated equity: 35-45% against his likely range

Pot odds: I'm getting 4000:1500 (2.67:1) to call, needing only ~27% equity to break even. I have well more than this.

Action decision: Calling is optimal here. I have:
1. Proper pot odds with multiple outs (flush draw is dominant)
2. Card equity against his likely value range
3. The flush draw provides excellent turn-to-river equity (roughly 35% to complete on river with 9 clean outs)
4. King kicker gives additional equity against many hands

Folding would be discarding a hand with +EV expected value. Raising would bloat the pot while potentially behind and give Minni correct odds to continue. Calling is the mathematically sound play.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♠J♥ on Jc 9c 2s 2d Kc), but the river K♣ is a very bad card for my range: it pairs the board and completes club draws. Claude called a sizable c‑bet on the flop and another large bet on the turn, which narrows his range to sticky hands and draws (Jx, 9x, medium pocket pairs, and club draws). Many of those holdings now beat me (trip 9s/Js, Kx, any two clubs → flush, unlikely quads 22), while the hands I beat (worse jacks, some 9x, lower pairs) often fold to river aggression. Betting thin value here is low EV — I’ll get called by the hands that beat me and fold out the worse hands I want action from. Pot control is the best line: check and go to showdown. Plan: check; if Claude fires a small river bet (thin value/bluff) I will call, but I will fold to a large raise representing K/flush/strong made hand.

I'm on the river with Ks9s on a board of Jc9c2s2d Kc. I have made two pair: Kings and Nines with a Jack kicker (KK99J).

Analysis of the situation:
1. Hand strength: Two pair is a solid hand, but given Minni's aggressive 3-betting preflop and betting through the flop and turn, his range likely contains hands that beat me:
   - AK: He'd have Kings and Nines with an Ace kicker (beats my Jack kicker)
   - AJ: He'd have Jacks and Nines (beats my two pair)
   - JJ: He'd have a full house (beats two pair)
   - AQ: I'd be ahead

2. River dynamics: Minni checked the river after betting aggressively on flop (700) and turn (1500). The King that hit the river likely helped stronger hands in his range (AK is very common in his 3-betting range), which explains why he checked - he may be looking to go to showdown rather than risk further escalation.

3. Value vs. risk: While I could theoretically bet for value, betting into his check when my hand is likely not the strongest in his range (given AK, AJ, JJ are all in his range and all beat me) would be negative EV. If I bet and get called, I'm likely losing additional chips to a better hand.

4. Showdown logic: Taking my two pair to showdown is the prudent play. I maintain my chip position if I'm behind and get the win if he was betting marginal hands.

Checking maximizes my expected value by avoiding costly mistakes.

I'm in UTG (early position) with Td3s - a weak, unsuited hand with poor connectivity. From UTG, I need to play a tight range of premium hands (premium pairs, broadway cards like AK/AQ, strong broadway combinations). Td3s with a weak three kicker doesn't meet this threshold. While a ten has some value, combining it with a three from early position is a losing play in the long run. I have position disadvantage and should preserve chips by folding weak hands in early position where I have no information about the strength of opponents' hands.

Folded to me on the button with AKo (Ah Kd), a premium hand in late position. Excellent spot to raise for value against the blinds' wide calling/folding ranges, and to frequently steal the dead money (pot of 150). Position allows control postflop. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is standard sizing: builds pot when ahead, applies pressure. Minni and Flash have shown some aggression but also passivity in spots; AKo crushes their calling ranges. Deep stacks support this play.

Facing a button open to 300 from my small blind with Ac4d: this hand is marginal but has the Ace-blocker and decent showdown/realization value. Calling 250 more (300 total committed including my 50 SB) keeps the pot small, lets the BB act (I can fold to an oversized 3-bet), and gives me a chance to realize equity out of position. A min-3bet here would be thin and I don't want to inflate the pot OOP with a weak A-x; folding is too tight and gives Elon too much credit. Plan: call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously — fold to big 3-bet, otherwise play flop for pot control or target streets where I improve.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 6h. Elon raised from the Button to 300, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. Although the pot odds are roughly 4.5:1, 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Calling here often leads to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated or forced to fold. Following a sound professional strategy, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

Check. With Ac4d on a Th Js 6c flop I only have Ace-high with a very poor kicker and effectively no meaningful backdoor equity — I can’t make a flush and the only realistic straight requires both K and Q to come. Elon’s button open is wide but on this flop his range contains many tens/jacks and broadway cards that currently beat me. Leading out OOP is unnecessarily risky and thin; I prefer to control the pot size and see how he reacts. Plan: pot control — check and fold to a sizable c-bet (exploitatively >~40% pot, ~≥280) since my equity is low; call smaller bets (~≤1/3 pot, ~≤230) to catch bluffs and re-evaluate on the turn. Don’t bluff-raise OOP with this hand.

Preflop raiser in position heads-up against SB caller, who has a wide and somewhat weak range after calling a button raise. Flop Th Js 6c misses my AKo entirely but connects decently with my range (AJs, KQs, AQo, ATs, small pairs that flop sets occasionally). Board has some straight draw potential (98o/s, T9s, 87s) but is relatively dry with no flush draws yet. Ace-high likely best hand or has live cards against better hands. Minni's check suggests weakness, but she could have Jx, Tx, or floats. Standard c-bet for thin value, to protect against overcards/draws, deny equity, and charge her range. Sizing 400 (~55% pot) allows fold equity while keeping pot manageable deepstacked; smaller risks calls, larger commits too much. Position lets me realize equity well postflop.

I called preflop to realize equity and checked the flop to control the pot. Elon bet 400 into the roughly 700 pot (pot now 1100) — a large c-bet that would cost me 400 to win 1500, so I’d need ≈26.7% equity to break even. With Ac4d I only have Ace-high with a weak kicker, no meaningful backdoor flush, and very few clean outs. Versus a BTN c-betting range (many J/T hands, broadways, some pairs and draws) my true equity is well under the required threshold. The Ace blocker helps a bit but not enough to justify calling out of position and facing tough turn decisions. This is exactly the sort of sizable c-bet I planned to fold to, so I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Ts6d is a trash hand: offsuit, poor high card, weak connectivity, dominated by better tens and sixes. UTG in a 4-handed game, but still early position, out of position post-flop against fields. Deep stacks, no reason to limp or raise this speculative hand. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I have 10♥2♠ on the Button — a very weak, offsuit, uncoordinated holding. Calling 100 into the 150 pot is cheap and buys position against the blinds; pot odds (risk 100 to win ~250) and positional advantage justify seeing a flop. Raising with this hand is suboptimal because Claude has shown willingness to defend/call and I don’t want to get called or 3-bet and be dominated postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to significant aggression when I miss, play cautiously if I pair the Ten (poor kicker), and leverage position to either take small pots or fold losing spots. Therefore I call 100.

I am in the Small Blind with Nine-Six offsuit. Minni limped from the button, and Elon has folded. While I am getting 5-to-1 pot odds to complete the bet, Nine-Six offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. I prefer to fold here and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than risking chips in a marginal spot against two opponents.

I'm in the big blind with Td3c (a weak, unconnected hand). The action has folded to me with only Minni remaining in the pot after calling from the button. Since I'm already in for the big blind with no additional bet to call, I should check and see the flop cheaply. Td3c has very limited value preflop - my best outcome is to hit a pair or find favorable board texture postflop where I'll have positional advantage. With such weak holdings, raising doesn't make sense as it will only fold out weaker hands or get called by better holdings. Taking a flop to realize my equity is the optimal strategy here.

I'm holding Td3c on a flop of Ah Qs Ac. This is complete air - no pair, no draw, no meaningful equity. The board is paired with aces, which is a dry, uncoordinated texture. 

My hand has zero connectivity to this board. Against Minni's likely range after calling from the button (which includes high cards, pairs, and broadway hands), I'm dominated. 

Checking is the correct play here because:
1. Bluffing on this dry board with paired aces is inefficient - it either folds out weaker hands I could win against later or gets called by better holdings
2. This hand is too weak to put money in with confidence
3. Checking allows me to realize some equity by seeing further streets if Minni checks back, or to fold cheaply if she bets and I don't improve

Preserving my stack with a check-and-fold plan is optimal given the weakness of my hand.

Check. I completely missed this flop (I hold 2s 10h vs A♥ Q♠ A♣). There are no realistic backdoor draws — at best I can pair the 10 or 2, which will frequently be second‑best. Claude checked the flop; given his prior tendencies (he’s shown he will call down and defend postflop), my fold equity as a bluff is limited and a bet will often get called by Ax/any pair. Pot control is the priority: I’ll check to see a free turn and fold to significant aggression rather than build a pot with virtually no equity. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (only continue if I hit or the board becomes very favorable); if he bets I will generally fold to a standard-sized bet.

I'm holding Td3c on a turn board of Ah Qs Ac 9s. This is complete air with zero equity - no pair, no draw, no connectivity whatsoever. 

Minni called from the button preflop and checked the flop, so she has some reasonable holdings but nothing that got there on the flop. The 9s on the turn doesn't improve my hand at all.

With such a weak holding, checking is the only reasonable option. I should take the free river card if Minni checks back, or fold immediately if she bets. Betting or raising with this trash hand makes no sense - I'm not winning if called and I'm not favored in a showdown against Minni's button calling range.

Preserving my stack by checking and evaluating her action on the river is the optimal play.

I hold T♥2♠ on Ah Q♠ Ac 9♠ — I only have a weak single pair (tens) with no legitimate draws. Claude has checked the flop and turn but has shown a tendency to defend and call down in prior hands, so my fold equity as a bluff is very low and I shouldn’t be building the pot. Pot control is the priority: check to see the river and preserve chips. I will fold to any sizable bet on the river (or the turn if he had bet); if he checks the river too I’ll re-evaluate only if I improve or get a clear, credible bluffing spot. For the turn the correct action is to check.

I'm holding Td3c on a river board of AhQsAc9sQd (a fully paired, uncoordinated board with two pair showing). This is complete air with zero equity.

Minni has been checking the entire way through (flop, turn, and now river), which indicates she either has a marginal/medium hand (like a queen, weak ace, or small pair) or completely whiffed. 

My options:
1. **Check**: Get to showdown for free, win if she also has nothing
2. **Bet**: Either get called by better hands (and lose), or fold out worse hands that would have checked anyway, reducing my win rate

On a river with a heavily paired, uncoordinated board like this, bluffing is inefficient. The board texture doesn't support a polarized betting range for me - I have nothing, and any bet just turns my hand into a pure bluff that wins against limited holdings.

Checking maximizes my equity by either reaching showdown against a missed hand or allowing Minni to value bet if she has something (where I'll fold). This is the mathematically optimal play with zero equity on the river.

Board is Ah Ac Qs Qd 9s — the five‑card board already makes Aces and Queens (A A Q Q 9). Checking on the turn with 9s5s on a paired board (2c-3s-3h-Qd). My hand is currently weak at 9-high with a flush draw (9 outs). Plan: fold to a significant 3-bet (or shove from Elon); if called, use position to c-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance when I miss. I am in the Small Blind with 52offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand given its low equity and the fact that I'd be playing the rest of the hand out of position. I will fold.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position in the big blind facing a button raise. Minni's button range is wide, but my hand is easily dominated by better kings and lacks strong equity or implied odds. Pot odds are about 2.25:1 (calling 200 into 450), but folding preserves my 57BB stack for better opportunities in a long 150-hand session. No need to defend this marginal spot.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of Eights (8s 8c). This is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand. Elon and Minni are in the blinds. A standard open from the button is appropriate here to either win the blinds or isolate one of the players.

9c3d is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated offsuit with low cards and no potential for straights or flushes. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands, calling or raising OOP from small blind risks tough post-flop spots with poor equity and playability. Pot odds don't justify calling (250 to call into 450 pot is ~36% implied, but hand too weak). Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I have A♦T♥ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 200 to call). Heads-up ATo is a very playable hand and too strong to fold. Versus a BTN open I want to both get value from worse Axs/Kx/broadways and apply pressure to a likely wide opening range. With deep stacks I prefer a standard 3‑bet sizing that gives meaningful fold equity but leaves room to play postflop — I’ll raise to 1200 total. If he folds I pick up the pot now; if he calls I can continue with position awareness (bet big when I hit an A or T, or play cautiously OOP with backdoor equity); if he 4‑bets large I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold ATo OOP to heavy aggression.

I hold pocket Eights (8s 8c) on the button. I opened to 300, and Minni in the Big Blind 3-bet to 1200. Minni has been playing fairly aggressively and showed some bluffs or semi-bluffs in previous hands (e.g., Hand #27). Calling allows me to play a pot in position and see a flop, where I can evaluate based on board texture. If the flop comes low, my hand is very strong; if overcards appear, I can play cautiously.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♦T♥ for thin value and fold equity. On this flop (K♦ 6♣ 9♦) I completely miss — I have only Ace‑high and a single diamond that gives only a backdoor possibility. Flash’s call of my 3‑bet indicates a fairly wide calling range that contains Kx, medium pairs and broadways; many of those hands will happily call a continuation bet. OOP with no pair and limited equity I prefer to control the pot and see a turn rather than barreling into resistance. Checking keeps weaker parts of his range honest, preserves fold equity on later streets, and allows me to fold to heavy aggression without committing a large chunk of my stack. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn — call small C‑bets to realize equity, fold to large, polarized bets.

I hold pocket Eights (8s 8c) in a 3-bet pot. The flop comes Kd-6c-9d, which contains one overcard to my pair. Minni, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked to me. This check often indicates either a 'give up' with a hand like AQ/AJ that missed, a pot-control move with a medium pair like JJ/TT, or occasionally a trap with a King. Given that I have showdown value and the board is somewhat coordinated (flush and straight draws possible), checking back is a solid play to control the pot size and realize my equity. If I bet, I mostly get called by better hands and fold out the bluffs I beat. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and can evaluate the turn in position.

I 3‑bet pre with A♦T♥ and checked the flop as planned to control the pot OOP. The turn J♠ changes very little for my hand: I still have only Ace‑high and the turn being a spade (not a diamond) kills the backdoor diamond plan — there’s no flush now. My only clean outs are pairing an A or T (3+3) and a Q (4) for a Broadway straight, but many of those river cards won’t be clean vs a Kx or made pair. Flash’s line (call 3‑bet, check flop) is consistent with a wide but often-made range (Kx, mid pairs, broadways) that will call a probe bet too often and fold too rarely to justify barreling OOP with just Ace‑high. Pot control and realization of equity is the right play: check and either call a small bet to see the river or fold to heavy aggression. If I improve on the river (pair or better) I can then extract value; otherwise I’ll preserve my stack.

On the turn Jack of Spades, the board is now Kd 6c 9d Js. Minni has checked for the second time in a 3-bet pot. My pocket Eights are now behind both the King and the Jack. Minni's range for 3-betting pre-flop includes hands like AK, KQ, KJ, JJ, TT, and potentially some diamond flush draws or straight draws like QTs. By checking back the flop, I kept the pot small. Minni's second check could still be a trap, but it's more likely a hand that is giving up or has medium showdown value like TT. Since the board is quite coordinated and I've fallen further behind in the range of hands she would likely 3-bet, I will check back again. This allows me to see a cheap river and potentially win at showdown if she was bluffing or holds a hand like 77/TT, while minimizing losses against her value range.

I will check to a showdown. I 3‑bet preflop with A♦T♥ for value and fold equity, then pot‑controlled by checking the flop and turn — I never improved and on the river I only have Ace‑high. Flash’s line (call 3‑bet, check flop, check turn) is consistent with a wide but often‑made final range (Kx, pairs, Jx, some broadways) that will call a river probe too often and fold too rarely for a bluff to be profitable. Betting here would be thin value at best (only gets called by worse Ax/Qx rarely) and will get called by nearly all hands that beat me; a small bluff has poor fold equity versus this caller. Checking preserves my stack and lets me realize the (small) showdown equity vs the parts of his range that missed. Action: check (amount 0).

I hold pocket Eights in a 3-bet pot. The board is Kd-6c-9d-Js-4s. Minni has checked on the flop, turn, and river after being the pre-flop aggressor. My hand has significant showdown value against her 'give-up' range, which includes hands like AQ, AJ, or missed diamond flush draws. However, there is no value in betting because almost no worse hand will call, and many better hands (like a King or a Jack that she is pot-controlling) will call or even check-raise. Checking back ensures I realize my equity and reach showdown for free in a pot that hasn't grown since the pre-flop call.

3-handed on the button with J8s suited connectors, a strong hand for position. Wide ranges pre-flop in short-handed play. SB likely folded given pot size of 150 (blinds only), BB checked. Raising to 300 applies fold equity to steal blinds, builds pot when ahead, and maintains initiative. Post-flop, suited connector has excellent implied odds, straight/flush potential, and position to realize equity. Standard sizing ~3x BB. Deep stacks allow flexibility.

Elon (button) opened to 300. I’m in the small blind with Ten of clubs (Tc) and Four of spades (4s) — a weak, offsuit, poorly connected hand that will be out of position postflop and is often dominated by a standard button opening range. Pot is 450 and it costs 250 to call (calling would need ~35.7% equity to break even), which T4o doesn’t have against an opener’s range. 3-betting as a bluff here is too thin given the big blind can still act and Elon has a healthy stack. With a long 150-hand session ahead, preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I'm in the Big Blind holding A2o. Elon (Button) has raised to 300, a sizing he has used consistently throughout the game. With Minni folding in the Small Blind, I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.25:1. While A2o is a weak Ace, it is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a standard Button opening range, even though I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. I will call to see a flop.

On a monotone board of 5h 8h Kh, my Ace of Clubs and Deuce of Diamonds have very little equity. I do not have a heart in my hand, meaning I have no flush draw and no protection against a fourth heart appearing on the turn. Since I am out of position against Elon, the standard play is to check. If he bets, I will likely fold as this board hits a button opening range well (containing many Kx and suited heart hands) and I have virtually no improving outs that I can feel confident in.

Flop of 5h-8h-Kh is extremely draw-heavy with a flush draw dominant and straight possibilities (e.g., 6-7). I have middle pair (eights) with a decent jack kicker and position. Half-pot sizing (around 325-400) balances value from worse pairs/draws while keeping pot manageable with deep stacks (my 5350 effective). Checking risks giving free cards to improve and loses value from the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace of Clubs and Deuce of Diamonds on a monotone board of King of Hearts, Eight of Hearts, and Five of Hearts. I raise to 300 from the button with K♥6♦. In a three-handed spot the button has a lot of initiative and K6o is worth using as a steal attempt — it plays reasonably well postflop in position and I want to avoid a multiway limp pot. A 300 sizing (about 3x the BB) is standard at this table, gives meaningful fold equity versus both blinds, and is large enough to discourage too many speculative calls but not so big as to commit me if I face aggression. Plan: if I get a single caller I'll play postflop with positional advantage (c-bet on favorable textures, pot-control on dry cards); if I face a sizable 3-bet, K6o is usually a fold out of position against strong aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h6h, a strong suited connector that plays well multi-way or in heads-up pots with deep stacks. Minni (Button) has raised to 300. My stack is deep (21,750), while Elon in the Big Blind is relatively short (5,900). This hand has good playability for semi-bluffing or hitting a monster flop. I will call and see what the Big Blind does.

6s4c is a very weak hand: low, gapped, offsuit effectively in playability despite being suited. Facing a button raise to 3BB and SB flat, their ranges are decently strong. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 into 700+200=900, wait pot becomes 900), but post-flop OOP with no initiative and poor playability, EV is negative. Better to fold and wait for stronger hands given my 59BB stack.

I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to Minni to see how she responds to the board. If she bets, I will evaluate the pot odds for my draw, but for now, checking is the most standard and disciplined play.

I opened to 300 preflop and got a single caller from the SB. Flop Ts-8s-4d is fairly dry with two spades; I have one overcard (K) and position. Flash’s calling range from the SB is wide (weak broadways, small pairs, suited connectors, some spades) and many of those hands will fold to a c-bet. With initiative and position I want to seize the pot now and deny equity to spade draws while folding out unpaired holdings. I’ll make a standard continuation bet of about half the pot to apply pressure without overcommitting. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check a blank, evaluate double-barrel vs passive floats, and fold to sizable raises — K6o has poor showdown value vs strong aggression). Bet 350 (~1/2 pot).

On a board of Ts 8s 4d, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Minni has made a small continuation bet of 350 into a 700 pot (offering 3:1 odds). While my 5-high and 6-high are extremely weak, the gutshot plus the potential to outplay the opponent on future streets makes calling a reasonable speculative play, especially with my deep stack. I am getting the right price to see if I can improve on the turn or potentially represent a stronger hand if the board develops favorably.

On the turn 7 of spades, I have completed a straight (4-5-6-7-8). However, the 7 of spades is a very coordinated card as it also completes a possible flush (there are now three spades on the board) and a higher straight (6-7-8-9-10) is possible if Minni holds a 9. Given the board texture and my position, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to the scare card. If she bets, I will most likely call to see the river, keeping the pot manageable in case I am already beat by a flush or a higher straight, while still getting value from her potential bluffs or lower two-pair hands.

Board is Ts-8s-4d-7s (third spade on the turn). I have K♥6♦ — no pair and no spade. Pot is 1400 and Flash (SB) called my flop c-bet, then checked the turn. His calling range on the flop is wide and contains lots of spade draws and small pairs; the 7♠ completes many of those draws. With no showdown value and very little fold equity on this wet/scary turn, a double-barrel is high-risk (I’ll often get called by completed spades or pairs). I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity vs bluffs, and re-evaluate on the river. Plan: if the river bricks non-spade I can consider a small river bluff/steal or check back for showdown depending on the card; if I face a meaningful bet on the river I will fold most of the time since K6 has poor showdown value vs a calling/checking line.

On the river, the board is Ts 8s 4d 7s 8h. I have a straight (4-5-6-7-8). The 8h on the river pairs the board, which is a concern if Minni has a full house (88, TT, 77, 44), but most of those are unlikely given the pre-flop and flop action. The bigger concern is the flush (three spades on board) or a higher straight (any 9). Minni checked back on the turn, which often indicates she doesn't have the flush or a super strong hand, or she is pot-controlling. Since I'm out of position and the board is very wet, checking is the safest way to get to showdown. If she bets, I will evaluate the size, but checking here to induce a bluff or see a cheap showdown with my straight is the optimal play.

Board: Ts 8s 4d 7s 8h. I hold K♥6♦ — no pair, no spade. Line: I raised pre, c-bet flop and Flash (SB) called, then checked the turn (third spade) and checked the river. His call on the flop followed by turn check narrows him to a mix of missed spade draws, weak broadways, and small pairs; he also can have some slow-played made hands (sets, two-pair, or a turned flush), but many of those would bet for value on the river or at least call a small bet. With essentially zero showdown value myself, checking guarantees losing most of the time; a small river bluff targets the portion of his range that folded to pressure (KQ/KJ-type hands, lone overcards, and missed floats). I size small — ~1/3 pot (450) — so the break-even fold frequency is only ~24% (450/1850), which is reasonable given his line. If he folds I pick up the 1,400 pot; if he calls I lose a small amount relative to my stack; if he raises I will fold. Betting here is a thin, positionally driven bluff to maximize fold equity rather than chasing showdown with a losing hand.

On the river, the board is Ts 8s 4d 7s 8h. I have a straight (4-5-6-7-8). The board is coordinated with three spades and a pair of eights. Minni checked back on the turn when the third spade arrived, which often suggests she doesn't have a flush or was playing cautiously. Her river bet of 450 into a 1400 pot is relatively small (about 1/3 pot). While I am vulnerable to a flush, a higher straight (any 9), or trips/full house (an 8 or pocket pairs), the pot odds offered (needing to be right about 20% of the time) make this a mandatory call with a straight. Raising is not advisable as I'm likely only called by hands that beat me.

I am on the button with 7h5h, a speculative but playable suited connector. The pot is small (150) and it only costs 50 more to complete the small blind or 100 to call the big blind if no one raised. 8s9h are suited connectors with good playability and potential to flop strong hands like straights, two-pair, or flush draws. Facing a button raise to 3x from Flash, who frequently raises light from late position based on hand history. Pot odds are favorable: calling an additional 250 into a 450 pot offers about 1.8:1 immediate odds, improving to better with implied odds given deep stacks (mine ~58BB, opponents deeper). OOP in SB but multi-way pot possible if BB defends, and hand flops well against a wide button range. Raising not ideal as it's not a premium 3-bet hand; folding too tight for speculative hand with position and odds.

I’m in the BB with A♥5♣ facing a button open to 300 and the SB called — pot is 700 and I need to put in 200 more. A5o is marginal and often dominated, but the price is good (200 into 700 ~ 22% equity needed) and I want to see a flop in a multiway pot. Raising to isolate is unnecessary with a weak kicker and will either bloat the pot or invite a 3-bet from Flash. Plan: call, see flop — if I hit an Ace I’ll extract value, otherwise be prepared to check-fold to large aggression or check-call small c-bets to realize equity.

Flop is extremely coordinated with three hearts to the King-high flush draw, plus broadway cards, making it a dream spot for many preflop calling ranges. My 9h8s flops a strong flush draw (9 clean outs to the nut flush if no higher hearts in opponents' ranges, plus backdoor straight potential with runner-runner 7-10 or 10-7). No pair or strong made hand, pure semi-bluff draw with ~35% equity heads-up but diluted multiway OOP. As SB first to act in 3-way pot (pot 900, my stack 5600 ~6x pot), donk betting is suboptimal: low fold equity vs. Flash's aggressive BTN range (often bets flops) and Minni's speculative BB defense; invites raises I'm committed to calling with draw. Better to check to aggressor (Flash), who will c-bet frequently here (value with top pair+, protection with draws/overcards, bluffs). Plan: check-call small/medium bets for odds/implied (~2:1 direct + deep stacks), check-raise big bets or vs stubborn opponent; check-through gives free card/turn equity. Preserves stack, realizes equity postflop where suited connectors shine. History shows Flash bets flops aggressively (e.g., Hands 2,10,16,27,28); Minni passive postflop.

Check. I have A♥5♣ on J♥–6♥–K♥: I do NOT have a made hand — just ace-high — but I hold the A♥ which is a very valuable blocker and gives me nine hearts to hit the nut flush (≈19% to hit on the turn, ≈35% by river). This is a three-way pot with the preflop aggressor and a caller, so there are many K/J/pair combos that currently beat me. Betting here would bloat the pot while often still being behind; checking controls the pot, preserves my flush equity, and lets me see how the BTN reacts. Plan: check and call small continuation bets to realize ~35% equity; fold to large aggression unless I improve. If checked through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue with pot-control or pot-build if I hit).

I have a flush draw on a monotone board (Jh 6h Kh) with 7h 5h. After both Elon and Minni checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor, I have the opportunity to take the pot down now or build it up for when I hit. A bet of 450 (half the pot) is a standard continuation bet that exerts pressure on hands without a heart while allowing me to semi-bluff with significant equity. If I am called, I still have roughly a 36% chance to make my flush by the river.

Flop remains highly coordinated with three hearts, confirming my strong nut flush draw (9h gives 9 clean outs to the K-high flush, plus backdoor straight potential with 7-10-T runners). Equity ~35-40% vs Flash's likely Button c-bet range (TPTK like Khx/Jhx, flush draws like Ahx/Qhx, weaker pairs, bluffs/overcards). Half-pot bet (450 into 900) from aggressor after two checks suggests wide range including value/protection/bluffs. Pot odds excellent: 450 to enter 1350 pot (~3:1, needing 23% equity). Implied odds strong with deep stacks (Flash 222+, Minni 108) for when we hit. Minni's check indicates weakness (passive history, unlikely strong made hand/draw that check-raises). Raising risks multiway commitment OOP or isolation OOP vs Flash, low fold equity vs his aggression (often calls/raises floats, e.g., Hands 9,10,16). Check-call realizes equity efficiently, keeps pot controlled, induces bluffs on turns/bricks, check-fold turns if misses/brick scary. Consistent with preflop plan; folding too nitty, raising too ambitious with pure draw OOP 3-way.

I have A♥5♣ on J♥–6♥–K♥: no pair but I hold the A♥ so I have the nut-heart blocker and a four‑to‑a‑flush (9 hearts left). Pot is 1,800 and it costs me 450 to call → I need ≈20% equity. My flush-only equity to the river is ≈35% (≈19% to hit on the turn), plus small additional equity from pairing the A or 5. Given it’s a multiway pot (BTN c-bet, SB called), raising here would bloat the pot with only a draw, risk a reraise, and reduce my ability to fold to serious aggression — Elon is relatively short (5,150) so a raise could force awkward shoves. Calling is the correct, +EV, pot‑control play: realize equity cheaply, see the turn, fold to heavy action if I miss, and extract value or barrel when I improve (bet for value if I make the flush, proceed cautiously on a mere pair).

Turn pairs the 6 (board: Jh6hKh6d), a blank for my nut flush draw (9h still 9 outs ~18% to hit river, no pair/set/straight). Pot 2250, first to act OOP 3-way with deep stacks (mine 5150 ~2.3x pot). Leading/donking pure draw has poor fold equity vs Flash's aggressive BTN range (likely continues betting TP/Jh/Kh/sets/draws, history of turn aggression e.g., Hands 10,16,20,23) and Minni's calling station tendencies; commits me OOP if raised. Better to check: realizes equity efficiently (direct pot odds + implied if hit), controls pot size, induces Flash's frequent bets (value/protection/bluffs on scary coordinated board), allows check-fold if overbet/brick while preserving stack for rivers we hit. Consistent with flop check-call plan exploiting position on aggressor. Folding equity absent without showdown value; betting risks spew. Check targets ~2:1+ odds on calls, induces mistakes postflop where suited connectors/draws excel multiway.

I have A♥5♣ on J♥–6♥–K♥–6♦: I missed my flush on the turn and now have only the Ace-high plus a four‑to‑the‑nut‑flush (9 hearts left → ~19.6% to hit the river). Pot is 2250 and Elon checked in front of me. Given the preflop/button aggression and a flop c‑bet that was called twice, it’s reasonably likely someone already has a made flush (two hearts in hand) or a strong pair/6 that turned trips — betting here would bloat a multiway pot with only a draw and often be called or raised by hands that currently beat me. I’ll control the pot and preserve my river equity: check. If Flash barrels a small bet I can call to see the river with ~20% to improve; I’ll fold to large-sized bets/shoves and re-evaluate on the river (value bet if I hit the heart or bluff selectively if the runout and blockers favor it).

The turn card is the 6d, which pairs the board but doesn't complete my flush. After my flop continuation bet was called by both Elon and Minni, the pot has grown significantly. Both opponents checked again on the turn. While another bet could potentially push out weak hands or one-heart holdings, the fact that both called the flop suggests at least one of them might have a made hand (like a King, Jack, or a slow-played flush) or a better flush draw. With my stack advantage and the free card offered by their checks, I will check behind to realize my equity. If I hit a heart on the river, I can value bet; if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff based on the final board and their actions.

River 9s pairs my 9h, giving me a pair of 9s with an 8 kicker in a 2250 pot, OOP first to act 3-way with stacks allowing play (5150). Board remains dangerous: hearts flush possible since flop (Flash's flop bet consistent with TP, flush, draws); paired 6s allows trips/full houses; K/J often in ranges beat me. Flash's preflop BTN raise + flop c-bet suggests wide range but continues with value/draws; turn check-through indicates possible medium strength/SDV or missed draw. Minni passive caller, unlikely to fold better. Pair of 9s has showdown value vs bluffs/weak pairs but thin value/no fold equity OOP vs aggressive Flash (huge stack) and calling Minni; betting risks calls from better (Kx, Jx, 66, K6, flushes ~30-40% of time) while check induces bluffs/check-behinds from missed draws/overpairs. Realizes equity efficiently, consistent with controlled postflop play on drawy board; folding pre-river would have been mistake, now pot control key. Check optimal.

Board: Jh–6h–Kh–6d–9s. I hold A♥5♣ and the river misses my flush and pairing outs — I only have Ace-high. Pot is 2,250 and it’s checked to me after Elon checked the river. Preflop/flow: Flash opened from BTN and c-bet the flop; both opponents called. Turn was checked around. That line is consistent with either a value hand exercising pot control (Kx, Jx, 6x, pocket pair) or missed/weak holdings slow-playing or settling for showdown.

Given it’s a three-way pot and I have no made hand, betting here as a bluff is low EV — I need both opponents to fold to make it profitable, and with Flash acting last he can call or raise light. My A♥ is a useful blocker to some nut-heart combos, but that doesn’t eliminate the many hands that already beat me (Ks, Js, 6x, two-pair/trips, etc.). A small river probe might fold out a few busted broadway combos, but it will also get called by worse one-pair hands and punished by raises. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a virtually worthless showdown hand.

Decision: check to take the cheapest route to showdown. If Flash then bets I’ll fold to meaningful sizing (I’m beat vs standard value/semibluff lines) and only consider calling tiny blocking bets from a known freezer — otherwise let stronger lines take the pot.

The board has missed my flush draw and I only have 7-high. After betting the flop and checking the turn, I have no showdown value. Both Elon and Minni have checked the turn and the river. While a bluff could potentially push them off weak pairs or missed draws, the presence of two opponents increases the risk that one of them is slow-playing a marginal hand or a Jack. Given my current stack lead and the fact that I've already invested in the pot, I'll check behind to see the showdown and avoid potentially losing more chips on a bluff that might be called by any pair.

On the button with Ac2c, suited ace in late position against blinds. A2s is a standard raising hand from the button due to position, blocker to AA/AK, suited for flush potential, and high equity realization post-flop. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard to steal blinds or build pot with initiative. Folding too tight, calling limping rare. Deep stacks allow post-flop playability.

I’m in the small blind with 2♠7♦ — essentially the worst non-pair starting hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button and Flash (big blind) is still to act with a big stack, so calling 250 to see a flop would leave me out of position against one or two players with very little equity or playability. Pot odds: calling 250 into the current 450 requires ~36% equity to break even, which 2‑7o doesn’t have versus an opener (and it’s worse multiway). A 3‑bet would be a pure bluff out of position and easy to punish. Fold and conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with T3s. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot is currently 450 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2.25:1 odds. While T3s is a weak hand, Elon's range from the button is typically very wide, and I have a significant stack advantage. However, T3s is likely dominated by much of his opening range and plays poorly out of position. Given Elon has a relatively short stack compared to the others (4850), I don't have a lot of implied odds to justify calling with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with K♥9♣ (K9o) — good spot to open. Pot is 150, to call 100. I want to seize position and build fold equity against the SB (Flash) and BB (Elon). Standard button open to ~3x the big blind (300) is appropriate: it’s big enough to discourage lots of blind defenders but small relative to stacks (Elon has 5,200; I have 10,300). Plan: isolate and play post‑flop in position; c‑bet on dry boards, proceed cautiously if facing a sizable 3‑bet (fold to large 3‑bets given K9o’s vulnerability, consider calling small 3‑bets depending on sizing and reads).

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven of spades, which is a strong starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I could 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot, calling is also a viable strategy to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop playability. Given that Elon is short-stacked in the Big Blind and might shove, calling allows me to see what he does first. However, a 3-bet to 1000 would often win the pot pre-flop or isolate Minni. Looking at previous hands, Minni is quite active. I will raise to 1000 to assert dominance and take the initiative.

5d3s is a complete trash hand with no playability, low cards, unsuited, disconnected. Facing a button open-raise to 300 (3x) and a small blind 3-bet to 1000 (over 3x the open), both opponents have strong ranges including premiums, broadways, suited connectors etc. Pot is 1400, calling 900 gives ~39% equity needed vs one but worse vs two strong ranges, and postflop I'll be OOP with a hand that rarely improves. No reason to continue, fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♥9♣ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. The pot is ~1400 and I need to put in 700 more (total committed 1000). K9o is marginal but has good post‑flop playability in position. The 3‑bet sizing is moderate and, given my positional advantage and implied odds against a wide/aggressive SB, calling is the best line. I’ll call to see a flop, plan to c‑bet on dry boards, and fold to heavy 4‑bet/river shove or clear turn aggression that I can’t credibly continue against. I won’t 4‑bet as a bluff — hand is too weak for that here.

On this T-4-2 flop with two spades, I have top pair kicker issues (Ace high) but a very strong nut flush draw. The pot is 2100 after I 3-bet pre-flop and Minni called. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet here to keep the initiative and maximize value for my draw while potentially winning the pot immediately. A bet of around 700 (1/3rd pot) is standard in 3-bet pots to keep ranges wide and put pressure on hands like mid-pairs or overcards that missed.

Call. Flash is a wide, aggressive SB 3‑bettor who continuation‑bets a small fraction of the pot; I only need ~20% equity to justify a call (700 into a ~2800 pot). K♥9♣ is just a single overcard here with no meaningful draws (with one club in my hand and one on the flop I cannot make a five‑card club flush by the river), so I won’t raise as a bluff. In position I can float and try to take the pot away on the turn if he shows weakness, or fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss. If I pair the K or 9 on the turn I can extract value; if the turn bricks and he checks I can barre lsmall to steal. Calling keeps the pot controlled while exploiting his wide c‑bet range.

On the turn, an Ace of diamonds appears, giving me top pair with my Ace of spades and maintaining my nut flush draw. The pot is currently 3500. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. This Ace is a very good card for my 3-betting range, and it likely improves me ahead of Minni's likely range of pocket pairs or tens. I should continue to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws she might have (though I block the nut flush draw). A bet of 1200 (roughly 1/3 pot) continues the narrative and extracts value from weaker Aces, Tens, or even mid-pairs that aren't ready to fold yet.

Facing a 1,200 bet into a 4,700 pot on Tc 4s 2s Ad with Kh9c in position. I’m getting ~20% pot odds to call (1,200 / 5,900), and Flash has shown a wide, aggressive 3-bet/c-bet profile — his range contains plenty of Axs, broadways and some bluffs. My hand has limited equity (I only improve by pairing the K or 9), no real draws, and I don’t have the blockers or fold equity to profitably bluff-raise. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize my equity in position, and lets me fold to heavy river aggression if I miss. Plan: call the 1,200; if I pair up on the river I can extract thin value, if he checks I can consider a small river steal on a blank, and if he fires a big bet on a blank I’ll fold.

The board has paired the Ace on the river, giving me trip Aces with a 7 kicker. My hand is extremely strong, though I do lose to better kickers (like AK, AQ, AJ) and potentially pocket tens or a full house if Minni had something like 44 or 22 pre-flop. However, Minni's calling on the flop and turn suggests she likely has a Ten, a smaller Ace, or perhaps a spade draw that just missed. Since the board is now AA-T-4-2, if she has an Ace, she's never folding, and if she has a Ten, she might call a small value bet. Checking might induce a bluff from missed draws, but given Minni's tendencies, value betting is more reliable. I will bet 1500, which is about 25% of the pot, to look like a blocking bet or to entice a call from a Ten or a weaker Ace.

Flash (SB) is a wide, aggressive 3‑bettor who c‑bet the flop (700), barreled the turn (1200) and now fires 1500 on the river. The pot is 7400; calling 1500 gives me ~20.3% pot odds (1500 / 7400). I hold K♥9♣ — no pair, only K‑high — so I only beat bluffs. However Flash’s line and relatively small bet sizes indicate a fair number of missed draws and thin bluffs in his range, plus value Ax combos that now make trips. I need only ~20% equity to justify a call and I believe his bluff frequency is high enough to make calling correct. Raising as a bluff is poor: I have no Ace blocker, almost no fold equity versus his value range, and a raise will often be called or re‑raised. Call 1500 to realize equity, keep the pot controlled, and preserve stack (if I call I’ll have ~5900 left).

I am on the button with 4s 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is folded to me, this hand has very little post-flop potential and I have been involved in several large pots recently, so I should maintain a solid image. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Preflop from SB heads-up vs BB with AsTh, a strong Ace-high hand with good playability. Flash has folded, so it's a good spot to raise for value and to steal the BB. Minni defends wide but ATo has good equity against her calling range and blocks AA/AK. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300 total). OOP limping is weak, folding too tight. Deep stacks allow for postflop play if called.

I’m in the big blind with A♥2♠ facing a standard open to 300. Calling 200 (total commit 300) is the right defensive play: pot odds are ~2:1 and A2o has decent raw equity and an Ace blocker versus a wide SB opening range. I’m out of position, so 3-betting a weak, offsuit ace is thin and folding is too tight given the odds. Plan: call to see a flop; if I hit an Ace I’ll continue for value/protection, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy, committed aggression when I have no improvement. This keeps my stack flexible and prevents giving up too much equity for a small price.

Preflop, raised ATo from SB as a strong ace-high hand with good playability against Minni's wide BB defending range. Flop 5h3sJc is a decent board for my range: low, somewhat coordinated but rainbow, with possible straight draws. I have ace-high, blocking AJ/Ax, no pair but top of range. OOP, but as preflop aggressor, a small continuation bet for thin value against her calling range (which includes worse aces, pairs like 66-TT, draws) and fold equity vs air/bluffs she might have. Pot 600, bet 2/3 pot (400) balances protection, value, and pot control. Minni has shown she folds to c-bets in similar spots (e.g., Hand 6, 29). Deep stacks, but this size commits little (~8% stack). Check risks giving free cards to draws or letting her bluff cheaply.

Call. Explanation: facing Elon’s 400 c‑bet into a ~1000 pot, I only need ≈28.6% equity to make a call correct (400/(1000+400)). With A♥2♠ on J♣‑5♥‑3♠ I have reasonable improvement odds: 3 Aces + 3 deuces + 4 fours = ~10 clean outs to the turn/river (≈21.3% to hit on the turn, ≈38–39% by the river). Elon opened from the SB wide and will c‑bet many bluffs and overcards I currently beat; I also block some Ace combos. Raising out of position with A2o is thin and will often get called by better hands (Jx, pairs) or commit too much of my stack; folding is too tight given the pot odds and equity. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable, realise equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I hit an Ace/4 (or pair the 2) I can play for value/raise; if I miss and face heavy action I’ll often fold.

On the turn, the board is 5h3sJc9d. My AsTh is still just ace-high with no pair or strong draw, though it blocks AJ and AK. Minni called my flop c-bet into a pot that favors my range (preflop raiser), so her range likely includes Jx (JQ, JT, suited connectors), mid pairs (66-88, TT?), draws that missed (like 46, 24 for wheel draws), or floats. The 9d turn is somewhat coordinating, completing straights like 78 and bringing some straight draws. Barreling OOP with air/thin value is low EV: better hands (Jx, pairs) likely continue, bluffs may raise, and I risk bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking achieves pot control, realizes equity by denying her free cards to improve (e.g., runner-runner draws or pairing), and allows her to bluff or value bet which I can hero-call profitably with my blocker and top-of-range equity. If she checks behind, good for pot control; if bets small, easy call; larger bet likely fold to avoid trouble. Deep stacks (my 4500 vs her 5200, pot 1400) favor playing post-flop cautiously here. Consistent with solver prefs: check back ace-high floaters OOP multiway-ish boards post-call.

Elon checked the turn into a pot of 1,400. I have A♥2♠ with no pair — only 6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 deuces), ≈13% to improve on the river — so my raw equity is low. A probe bet would need an opponent fold frequency of bet/(pot+bet) (e.g. a 400 bet needs ~22% folds) to be +EV; given Elon checked the turn his range is likely pot‑control/top‑pair/medium hands that will call a small probe more than that. Also with stacks ~5200 (me) vs 4500 (him) a larger bet risks a shove I can’t profitably call with air. Checking controls the pot, realizes my equity, keeps bluffs in his range, and lets me respond to the river card (bet for value if I hit an A/2, fold to heavy action if I miss). I’ll check behind and re‑evaluate on the river.

River Td pairs my Th, giving me pair of tens with ace kicker, a strong showdown value hand now improved from ace-high. Minni's range after calling flop c-bet on 5h3sJc (low coordinated board) and checking turn 9d includes Jx (top pair), mid pairs (66-99, TT?), missed draws (46 wheel, suited connectors), floats. Tens beats worse Ax, KQ, lower pairs, bluffs. Loses to sets, two-pair, straights (78). But as preflop/flop aggressor with position history and turn check inducing possible weakness, value betting thin is +EV: she calls too wide based on history (e.g., Hand 20 calls down light). Td also completes her possible draws but improves some marginal hands we beat. Bet 1000 (~70% pot) balances value from worse pairs/draws, fold equity vs air, pot control OOP. Her stack 5200, pot to 2400 commits her reasonably. Checking risks free card (unlikely) or her betting bluffs we snap, but misses value from calling range. Deep effective stacks favor extracting.

I have A♥2♠ on Jc‑5h‑3s‑9d‑Td — no pair, just Ace‑high. Elon bets 1,000 into the displayed pot (pot shown as 2,400). Calling 1,000 requires roughly 1,000/(2,400+1,000) ≈ 29.4% equity to break even. Consider Elon's line: open from the SB, c‑bet the flop, check the turn and now a small river bet. That line is weighted toward made hands (Jx, Tx, 9x, some two‑pair/combos) and medium strength hands that prefer pot control on the turn and a thin value probe on the river. There are bluffs in his range, but they are limited (missed broadways, maybe some suited connectors); my Ah actually removes a number of Ax bluff combos. My actual chance of being ahead with only Ace‑high against his likely value‑heavy range is well below the ~29% threshold. Raising as a bluff is too risky — he has many call/raise combos and a raise would commit a large portion of my stack unnecessarily. Given the pot odds, range reading, blocker effects and stack preservation over the long run, folding is the correct, +EV play here.

6d4h is a trash hand: offsuit, poorly connected, minimal equity against any calling or 3-betting range. Position on button offers some stealing potential, but this hand plays poorly post-flop even if successful, as it rarely makes strong hands and is easily dominated. Opponents' stacks are deep enough to defend, and history shows they call raises frequently. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for a limp, and raising commits too much with such a weak hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I'm in the small blind with T♦8♣ and 51bb effective facing only the big blind (Flash). This is a marginal, playable hand heads-up but it's easily dominated and I will be out of position postflop. Flash has shown frequent aggression and large stacks, so a min-raise to 200 would invite 3-bets or larger pots I'd rather avoid with this hand. I'll complete the blind by calling the extra 50 (total 100) to see a flop cheaply, preserve stack depth, and play a controlled postflop strategy: check/control and fold to large, disciplined aggression if I miss; play aggressively only if I pair the ten or pick up strong draws.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. Minni (Small Blind) has limped in. I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise. Given the relatively shallow effective stacks (Minni has about 51 big blinds) and the fact that K2o is a weak hand to play in a bloated pot out of position, checking is the most standard and safest play here. I have a massive stack advantage over both opponents, so I don't need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands pre-flop. I'll take the free flop.

Heads-up on the flop with T♦8♣; pot 200, board A♥ 9♠ 2♦. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draws — flush is impossible (only Td and 2♦ are diamonds so I can’t make five diamonds), and any straight requires runner-runner. Flash is a deep, aggressive big blind who checked preflop; betting into him with pure air here is -EV and will often get raised or put me in tough spots out of position. My objective is pot control: check and see the turn. If Flash bets, I will fold to sizable pressure and only consider calling very small probes that give the correct price to see the turn; I’ll only get aggressive if I pair the Ten or pick up a real draw on later streets.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker on a dry Ace-high board. Minni checked to me in a limped pot. There's no reason to bet here as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands like an Ace or a Nine, and I have some showdown value against her range. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see a turn for free, potentially improving or reaching a cheap showdown.

Check. I have T♦8♣ on A♥ 9♠ 2♦ Q♠ — no pair, no real draw and very little showdown equity. Flash checked both flop and turn, which more often indicates weakness or a slow-play; given his massive stack and aggressive tendencies I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with air. Pot is small (200) so pot-control is priority: check and see the river. I will fold to any sizable bet on the river and only continue to very small probes that give correct odds; I may consider a targeted river bluff only if the card and betting line credibly represent a hand I could have and the sizing is small.

I have bottom pair on a board that now contains an Ace and a Queen. Minni has checked for the second time in a limped pot. While my deuces have some showdown value, they are very weak. However, there is no need to bet; I am unlikely to get called by worse or fold out better. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see the river for free.

The river pairs my Ten giving me second pair (T♢8♣) on A♥ 9♠ 2♢ Q♠ T♠. Flash checked flop and turn, which more often signals weakness or a slow-play, but he’s an extremely deep, aggressive player who can check strong hands. My hand has showdown value versus bluffs and many missed draws, but is crushed by Ax, Qx, two pair, sets, and some ten-combinations. The pot is tiny (200) relative to effective stacks, so betting here has poor fold equity and will often get called by better hands or fold only worse. Best line is to take the cheap showdown: check and see a showdown. If Flash fires a very small probe I’ll consider calling for thin value; I’ll fold to any substantial river raise given my vulnerable kicker and the pot odds relative to his range.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker. The board has multiple overcards (Ace, Queen, Ten, Nine) and potential straights (any Jack). Minni has checked every street after limping pre-flop. In a limped pot that has been checked through to the river, my pair of deuces has very thin showdown value, but there is no merit in betting. If I bet, she will only call with hands that beat me (like a Nine, a Ten, or a weak Ace) and fold her air. By checking, I get to see if my pair is good in a small pot without risking more chips.

Three-handed, the button is very aggressive — open-raising a wide range is +EV. K♣5♠ is marginal but gains a lot from position and fold equity. I’ll raise to a standard 3x size (300) to seize the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and either take the pot preflop or play postflop in position. I’ll fold to a large 3-bet and proceed with c-bets on favorable, dry flops or control the pot if called.

In Hand #39, I am in the Small Blind with King of Spades and Eight of Diamonds. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While King-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand, we are playing three-handed and I am getting reasonable pot odds to see a flop. However, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who has shown aggression in previous pots. Given the stack depths and my position, a fold is a disciplined play to avoid difficult spots with a mediocre hand, though calling is also defensible. I will opt to fold and wait for a better spot.

9h5d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability postflop, no straight or flush potential worth defending. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in prior hands, my BB defending range shouldn't include this. Pot odds of calling 200 into 450 offer ~4:1, but equity against a wide button range is insufficient (~20-25% at best, often dominated). Folding preserves my 5800 stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game.

I am on the button with T9o in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead over Elon and Minni. Opening from the button with T9o is standard to apply pressure on the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size to take control of the pot.

5c8h is a trash hand offsuit with poor connectivity and low card value. Facing a button raise from Flash, who frequently raises wide from late position based on history. Defending blinds with this hand is -EV due to poor equity and playability postflop. Pot odds are ~1.8:1 (calling 250 into 450), requiring too much equity for this holding against a wide button range. Minni may defend or fold behind, but folding preserves my 5750 stack for stronger spots in a long 150-hand session.
